ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific satay sauce entry; closest match is peanut sauce (FOODON_03301710). Satay sauce is a spiced peanut-coconut milk condiment specific to Southeast Asian cuisine.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific satay sauce code; A00GG (Sauces and condiments) is the closest defensible broader category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for satay sauce as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of satay sauce as a dipping condiment is approximately 2 tablespoons (30 g), as used alongside satay skewers at a hawker stall.

nutrient_source.title: Satay Sauce — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Satay Sauce (cr_id F10040002), confidence 0.95. All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are taken verbatim from the HPB record.

gi_source.title: Peanut sauce / legume-based condiment GI analogous estimate — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for satay sauce in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 20 is estimated by analogy to peanut-based foods and legume-based sauces, which consistently show GI values in the 14–25 range due to high fat, protein, and fibre content slowing glucose absorption. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (20 × 3.894) / 100 = 0.78. Carbohydrate per 30 g serving = 12.98 × 0.30 = 3.894 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 313 mg/100 g and saturated fat ~6.5 g/100 g from coconut milk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit satay sauce to 1 tablespoon (15 g) per serving to keep sodium contribution below 50 mg and saturated fat below 1 g; request reduced coconut milk preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 11.59 g/100 g; however, GI is low (~20) and dietary fibre 4.19 g/100 g partially attenuates postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict satay sauce to 1 tablespoon (15 g) per occasion to limit added sugar to approximately 1.7 g per serving, and prefer preparations with reduced palm sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~6.5 g/100 g from coconut milk raises LDL cholesterol risk; peanuts provide beneficial MUFA and PUFA
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should use satay sauce sparingly (1 tablespoon / 15 g) to limit saturated fat intake, and favour preparations using reduced coconut milk to lower the saturated fat burden while retaining the cardioprotective unsaturated fats from peanuts.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat ~6.5 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g RA threshold; sugar 11.59 g/100 g approaches the 15 g inflammatory threshold; chilli (nightshade) may trigger joint flares
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit satay sauce due to pro-inflammatory saturated fat from coconut milk and added sugar; if consumed, restrict to 1 teaspoon (5 g) and request reduced coconut milk and sugar in preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Satay sauce (沙爹酱; Sos satay) is a thick, rich peanut-based dipping condiment that is a defining accompaniment to satay skewers in Singaporean and Malaysian hawker cuisine. It is made by blending ground roasted peanuts with coconut milk, lemongrass, galangal, chilli, shallots, garlic, palm sugar, tamarind, and salt, then simmering the mixture until it reaches a thick, glossy consistency. The flavour profile is complex: nutty, mildly spicy, slightly sweet from palm sugar, and tangy from tamarind, with a creamy richness from coconut milk. The texture is thick and coating, designed to cling to grilled meat skewers. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 240 kcal, 8.33 g protein, 17.2 g fat (of which approximately 6.5 g is saturated fat from coconut milk), 12.98 g carbohydrate, 11.59 g sugar, 4.19 g dietary fibre, and 313 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 20 (low), reflecting the high fat, protein, and fibre content that slows glucose absorption. Despite its low GI, the high sugar content (11.59 g/100 g) and saturated fat make it a condiment to be used in small portions by individuals managing diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. It is halal, plant-based, gluten-free, and dairy-free. In Singapore, satay sauce is served at hawker centres and food courts as a standard accompaniment to chicken, beef, or mutton satay, and is also used as a dipping sauce for ketupat (compressed rice cakes) and cucumber.
common_user_queries: What is satay sauce made of?
How many calories in satay sauce?
Is satay sauce suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of satay sauce?
How much sodium in satay sauce?
Can I eat satay sauce if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F10040002) with confidence 0.95. The food is a well-documented Singaporean hawker condiment with an exact match in the primary regional source. GI is an analogous estimate from peanut-based food analogues in the University of Sydney GI Database, which introduces minor uncertainty but does not affect the overall high confidence in the nutritional profile.
notes: Saturated fat of 6.5 g/100 g is estimated from the known composition of coconut milk in satay sauce preparations; the HPB source reports total fat (17.2 g/100 g) but does not disaggregate saturated, monounsaturated, and polyunsaturated fat fractions. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid fractions and micronutrient data for this traditional preparation are not reported in the HPB Foodo database or the USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent satay sauce entry as of April 2026. GI value of 20 is an analogous estimate based on peanut-based legume condiments in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for satay sauce was identified in peer-reviewed literature. Glycemic load per serving (30 g) = (20 × 3.894) / 100 = 0.78 g.
